title: Endogenous Multihoming and Network Effects: PlayStation, Xbox, or Both?
abstract: Competition between firms that sell incompatible varieties of network products can be fierce because it is important for each of them to attract a large number of users. The literature, therefore, predicts that stronger network effects decrease prices and profits. We show that this prediction hinges critically on an implicit or explicit assumption that each consumer buys only one of the varieties available in the market (singlehoming consumers). We show that multihoming (some consumers buying more than one variety) can arise endogenously if the number of exclusive features that each variety offers is sufficiently large. In sharp contrast to the conventional prediction under consumer singlehoming, we further show that both prices and profits can increase with the strength of the network effects if (some) consumers multihome. However, this does not necessarily imply that profits are higher under multihoming than under singlehoming. On the contrary, multihoming can constitute a prisonerвҖҷs dilemma for the firms in the sense that they could make higher profits if each consumer bought only one of the varieties.

article.1. Introduction: A product exhibits positive network effects if the utility a user derives from it increases with the number of other consumers using the same or a compatible product. We consider competition between two platforms that provide incompatible varieties of a network product (platform-specific network effects). Each consumer may choose to buy only one of the varieties (singlehoming) or both (multihoming), depending on what generates the higher net utility. Opening for endogenous multihoming has surprising effects on market equilibrium and platform pricing strategies. The assumption that all consumers singlehome, as is common in most of the literature, can generate highly misleading policy and management advice. The archetypical example of a network effect is one in which consumers directly benefit from communicating with each other as in telecommunications. Similar network effects are present in the market for video game consolesвҖ”in which Microsoft (Xbox) and Sony (PlayStation) are the main providersвҖ”because gamers now play online. A user of PlayStation, for instance, benefits if the pool of PlayStation users becomes larger. Network effects also arise if individual users of a product generate data that, for example, machine learning algorithms can utilize to increase the perceived quality of the product for other users. Another example is the recommendation systems used extensively by, for example, streaming services such as Netflix and HBO (movies/series) and Spotify and Apple Music (music).1On Spotify, users can share their playlists or create playlists together. A large quantity of user data is required to build good recommendation systems, but they can potentially become highly efficient. In 2017, around 70% of the videos watched on YouTube were recommended by the companyвҖҷs algorithms.2Consumer reviews are considered one of AmazonвҖҷs most important features and were introduced by the company as early as in 1995 (Chen and Xie 2008). Much of the literature that analyzes the interaction between network effects and pricing decisions uses discrete choice models (such as the Hotelling model) and presupposes that each consumer buys at the most one variety of a network product (i.e., all consumers are singlehomers).3A main result in this literature is that network effects may intensify competition and reduce prices and profits if different firms offer incompatible varieties. For many products and services, at least a fraction of the consumers multihome, and we show that this may turn the consequences of network effects on prices and profits upside down. Digitization has made multihoming easier for the consumers in industries such as media and entertainment services. Consumers may benefit from buying more than one variety if this generates an incremental value for them. For instance, consumers need to subscribe to both HBO and Netflix to have access toGame of Thrones(HBO) as well as toThe Crown(Netflix). In 2020, 20% of Netflix subscribers in the United States also subscribed to HBO Max, and 80% of HBO Max customers subscribed to Netflix.4Multihoming is also quite common in the gaming industry, in which, at the household level, almost half of Xbox One users own a PlayStation 4.5Both Microsoft and Xbox provide exclusive games on their consoles. Such exclusives give rise to an incremental value of buying an Xbox in addition to a PlayStation and vice versa. Additionally, the presence of network effects gives rise to an incremental value of becoming a multihomer. This is understood by the gaming community. In a post onReddit.com, for instance, both exclusives and network effects are highlighted when stressing the benefit of having Xbox as well as PlayStation:6вҖңI own both an Xbox One X and a PS4 Pro. The benefit is being able to play any console exclusives you want. As for downsides, aside from the initial cost, there are none. ItвҖҷs also nice to be able to play various games with different friends who have picked one or the other.вҖқ This quote summarizes the driving forces in the present paper. We consider a two-stage game in which platforms first announce product prices and, thereafter, consumers make their purchasing decision. With network effects, the perceived consumer value of a product variety increases with the number of other users of the product. In the basic model, we assume that consumers have responsive beliefs, meaning that they use announced prices as an input factor when forming their beliefs about the number of users of each variety (see further subsequent discussion). All other things being equal, a platform that charges a lower price than its rival is expected to attract a greater number of consumers. We show that, if some consumers choose to multihome, the presence of platform-specific network effects may increase both prices and profits. Think of HBO and Netflix, for which a fraction of the consumers multihome. Assume that the services at the outset exhibit no network effects but that both HBO and Netflix later introduce some features that give rise to small but positive platform-specific network effects (e.g., a recommendation system that helps consumers to choose the right movies). Demand for each variety will then increase, potentially resulting in both higher prices and profits. However, profits do not unambiguously increase with the strength of platform-specific network effects. Rather, we show that the relationship may become negative if the strength of the network effects goes beyond a critical level. The intuition is that the stronger the network effects, the more important it is to have a large consumer base. If the network effects become sufficiently strong, consumers care more about joining a large network than having their intrinsic favorite product. This intensifies the competitive pressure. We, thus, show that platform prices and profits are hump-shaped functions of the strength of the network effects. The number of exclusive features (attributes) that each variety offers is crucial for the equilibrium outcome. If the number of exclusives is large, a multihoming equilibrium exists. This is because providing more exclusives increases the consumersвҖҷ incremental value of buying a second variety. As emphasized earlier, most of the literature assumes that all consumers singlehome with no possibility of multihoming. This is troublesome because, as we show, an equilibrium in which all consumers singlehome only exists if the number of exclusives is sufficiently small. This demonstrates that management practice and policy should not generally be based on the standard outcomes from the literature on network economics. The higher the number of exclusives and the stronger the network effects, the more profitable multihoming becomes in relation to singlehoming. A combination of many exclusives and strong network effects implies that multihoming is a unique equilibrium such that platformsвҖҷ profits are higher than under singlehoming. In contrast, if the number of exclusives is small, multihoming may constitute a bad equilibrium such that platformsвҖҷ profits would have been higher in a counterfactual outcome in which all consumers singlehome. A main prediction from the scarce literature that allows for multihoming is that prices are strategically independent if some consumers choose to multihome in equilibrium (Kim and Serfes 2006,Anderson et al. 2017). We show that this independency result breaks down even when the platform-specific network effect is minimal. Prices will then be strategic complements under multihoming as well. Hence, our results may also have relevance for markets with relatively weak network effects (as might be the case for, e.g., streaming services). We provide three extensions of the basic model. First, we allow the platforms to invest to increase the number of exclusives before they compete on price. Thereby, the number of exclusives is endogenously determined. Our main results hold; within-platform network effects can increase both prices and profits. Second, we allow for compatibility between the platformsвҖҷ products, and this gives rise to cross-platform network effects.7If all consumers singlehome, the literature provides a clear managerial implication: make the products perfectly compatible if the platforms are symmetric. In sharp contrast, we find that the platforms should aim for zero or imperfect compatibility under partial multihoming, depending on the strength of the network effects. Perfect compatibility is never optimal under partial multihoming. Our third extension is to analyze whether our prediction that prices and profits may be higher with than without network effects hinges critically on the assumption that consumers use the announced prices when they form their expectations about platform sizes (responsive beliefs). The answer is no; the prediction holds even when the consumers do not take the announced prices into account when they infer how many consumers will buy each product (i.e., if the consumers have passive beliefs). Indeed, with passive beliefs, we find that prices and profits monotonically increase with the strength of the network effects under partial multihoming. The rest of the paper is organized as follows: InSection 2, we review related literature, andSection 3develops the model of network effects when allowing for multihoming. InSection 4, we provide the extensions described, whereasSection 5concludes.
article.2. Related Literature: The focus on consumer multihoming has mainly taken place within the literature on two-sided markets, in which cross-group network effects are the driving mechanism (Ambrus et al. 2016,Anderson et al. 2018,Athey et al. 2018,Belleflamme and Peitz 2019,Bakos and Halaburda 2020, among others). In contrast, we consider within-platform network effects, and combine elements from the substantial network economics literature and the more limited literature that allows for multihoming consumers (Kim and Serfes 2006,Anderson et al. 2017). The literature on network effects focuses on (i) technology adoption decisions (Farrell and Saloner (1985)andKatz and Shapiro (1986)are early contributions), (ii) compatibility decisions (Katz and Shapiro 1985,Farrell and Saloner 1992, and subsequent papers), and (iii) competitive pricing strategy decisions among providers of incompatible products or services. Our focus is on the latter, that is, pricing decisions of platforms that provide incompatible products, and we allow the consumers to select themselves into singlehomers and multihomers (as emphasized earlier, we consider compatibility in an extension). Within the Hotelling framework,Farrell and Saloner (1992),Shy (2001), andGrilo et al. (2001), among others, show that network effects may intensify price competition between providers of incompatible products (given that consumers have responsive beliefs).8Analogously,Katz and Shapiro (1985)show how network effects intensify competition under Cournot competition. A crucial presumption in these articles is that all consumers singlehome. However, as noted, singlehoming may not constitute an equilibrium. Our model has some similarities with the work ofDoganoglu and Wright (2006), who employ a Hotelling framework in which consumers may multihome to gain maximum benefit from the network effects. In contrast to our model, however, they do not endogenize multihoming. In their model, there are two groups of consumers; one group perceives the marginal network benefit as being small and the other large. The latter group is assumed to multihome. With network effects, consumer expectations about platform sizes play a crucial role. In the basic model, we assume that consumers use announced prices when they form their beliefs about platform sizes (responsive beliefs). In an extension, we consider the case in which consumer expectations are formed prior to the platforms setting prices (as inKatz and Shapiro 1985; see alsoGriva and Vettas 2011,Hurkens and LГіpez 2014).9The latter is commonly labeled passive expectations. In either case, we find that stronger network effects can increase prices and profits under partial multihoming. In both the basic model and the extension, we assume that consumers are rational.

article.5. Concluding Remarks: This paper shows that network effects have markedly different impacts on market performance in the case of partial multihoming than in the case of singlehoming. Our model determines the extent of multihoming endogenously as consumers select themselves into those who buy only one of the two products and those who buy both. A large strand of the literature simply assumes complete singlehoming, which can generate highly misleading predictions of how competition works in network industries. In our model, the strength of the network effect is exogenous and identical for both firms. Given our results, it is clear that firms may have incentives to choose technologies that allow for someвҖ”but not too strongвҖ” network effects. An interesting question is, therefore, how and to what degree firms can influence the size of the network benefit for consumers. Another is whether they can implement strategies that can prevent multihoming from arising in equilibrium in cases in which singlehoming would yield higher profits (see alsoAthey and Morton 2022)for a similar discussion in the context of two-sided markets). We leave these questions to future research. It is beyond the scope of the present paper to present a full-fledged welfare analysis, but we find it interesting to note that the existence of some network effects is in the common interest of both consumers and platforms. The consumers benefit because network effects increase both the intrinsic and the incremental values of the products, and the platforms benefit because the higher consumer value results in higher demand and higher prices. It is only if the network effects become sufficiently strong that prices fall and the platforms are harmed. This contrasts sharply with the outcome under singlehoming with covered markets in which network effects unambiguously hurt the platforms.
